candidates.title: Competitive Geometry of Feature Absorption: LV Unsupervised Detector + Corpus PMI + Downstream Impact

collisions.paper: Chanin et al. (2024). A is for Absorption: Studying Feature Splitting and Absorption in Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2409.14507. NeurIPS 2025 Oral.
collisions.overlap: Establishes qualitatively that corpus co-occurrence drives absorption. Uses the first-letter task as ground truth. The PMI causal mechanism is described qualitatively. Proposal's H2 must be positioned as the first QUANTITATIVE prediction test, not the discovery of the mechanism.

collisions.paper: Karvonen et al. (2025). SAEBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2503.09532. ICML 2025.
collisions.overlap: Provides 200+ SAEs and multiple downstream metrics (RAVEL, SCR, TPP, absorption). Notes qualitatively that proxy metric gains do not translate to downstream performance. Does NOT run pre-specified Pearson correlation between absorption scores and downstream tasks with Bonferroni correction. Component 3 remains novel in its systematic, pre-registered form.

collisions.paper: LessWrong (2024). Looking for Feature Absorption Automatically. lesswrong.com/posts/z7iyek97dAeQMxdSd/
collisions.overlap: Same goal: unsupervised absorption detection. Proposes detecting latents with similar causal effects on downstream layers but no co-occurrence. Reports NEGATIVE RESULT. Method uses ablation effect vector similarity — distinct from LV coefficient (decoder cosine + co-activation statistics). Negative result supports LV novelty.

collisions.paper: DeepMind Safety Research (2025). Negative Results for Sparse Autoencoders On Downstream Tasks. Medium blog, March 26, 2025.
collisions.overlap: Reports SAE 1-sparse probes underperform dense linear probes on harmful intent detection. Motivates H3 but does not run systematic absorption-rate correlation across 200+ SAEs.

candidates.differentiation_notes: H1 (LV detector): No paper applies Lotka-Volterra competitive exclusion to SAE features. The specific ratio form alpha_ij = sigma_ij * (f_j/f_i) and sharp threshold prediction are novel. H2 (corpus PMI): Reframe as first quantitative prediction test — corpus mechanism is known qualitatively. H3 (downstream): Pre-registered systematic correlation with Bonferroni correction across 200+ SAEs is novel. H4 (distributed absorption): DAS(k=3) metric and width paradox resolution via distributed competitive exclusion are novel.
critical_actions: Reframe H2 claim: 'first quantitative test of corpus co-occurrence hypothesis' not 'reframing absorption as data-driven'
Verify arXiv:2604.06495 exists and check if it contains PMI-based absorption prediction
Check SAEBench v4 supplementary for any pre-existing absorption-downstream correlation analysis
In paper, explicitly distinguish LV coefficient from SAEBench's cosine-similarity latent-finding step

candidates.title: The Absorption-Sparsity Phase Transition: An Information-Theoretic Theory

collisions.paper: Tang et al. (2025). A Unified Theory of Sparse Dictionary Learning: Piecewise Biconvexity and Spurious Minima. arXiv:2512.05534.
collisions.overlap: Provides formal theoretical framework for SAEs explaining feature absorption via non-identifiability of SDL optimization. Uses biconvex theory. Closer to cand_b's theoretical framing than the front-runner's LV approach.

collisions.paper: Wu et al. (2025). Interpreting and Steering LLMs with Mutual Information-Based Explanations on SAEs. arXiv:2502.15576.
collisions.overlap: Uses mutual information for SAE feature explanation quality. Not for absorption detection or absorption rate prediction.

candidates.differentiation_notes: Backup for H1 failure. Must differentiate from Tang et al. by emphasizing explicit (rho, L0, q) phase diagram and empirical validation on Gemma Scope. Information-theoretic A(P,C) = I(f_P; f_C)/H(f_P) as absorption predictor is not in Tang et al. or Wu et al.

candidates.title: Absorption at Scale: Empirical Scaling Laws and Iso-Absorption Curves

collisions.overlap: SAEBench already provides systematic comparison across 200+ SAEs at varying widths, L0, and architectures. Some scaling trends are visible in SAEBench figures. Regression formula and iso-absorption curves must add clear value beyond what SAEBench presents.

collisions.paper: Chanin et al. (2024). A is for Absorption. arXiv:2409.14507.
collisions.overlap: Fig 7b shows qualitative absorption vs. SAE size trend. Quantitative regression adds value but incremental.

candidates.differentiation_notes: Cross-domain validation (country names, given names) and explicit iso-absorption curves are novel aspects. Not sufficient as standalone paper without positive results on regression R^2 > 0.70. Use only if both H1 and H2 fail.

candidates.title: Does Absorption Reduction Help? Controlled Causal Experiments and Downstream Impact

collisions.overlap: SAEBench provides the data and some meta-analysis. The controlled L0-matching approach adds methodological rigor not present in SAEBench. Pre-specified predictions are a genuine contribution.

candidates.differentiation_notes: Component 3 of the front-runner already implements the core contributions of cand_d. Do not pursue as separate pivot unless front-runner Component 3 yields inconsistent results requiring clean methodological re-framing.

prior_art_registry.paper: Li et al. 2024 Geometry of Concepts
Chanin et al. Toy Models LessWrong
